News.title: Tributes left at scene where newborn baby girl died after falling from height – after woman, 43, arrested on ‘murder’
News.content:
TRAGIC SCENE Tributes left at scene where newborn baby girl died after falling from height – after woman, 43, arrested on ‘murder’

MOURNERS have paid tribute to an 18-day-old baby who died after falling from a height.

Flowers and a toy were left at the young girl’s London home as police investigate the tragic incident that happened on Saturday.





A 43-year-old woman has been arrested on suspicion of murder and has been taken into police custody.

Detective Chief Inspector Alison Foxwell said: “Our thoughts today are with the baby’s family.

“The death of an infant is always an extremely tragic occurrence, and officers are making urgent inquiries around the circumstances.

“At this stage, we believe the incident occurred within a domestic context, but we are appealing for anybody with information to come forward.”

Emergency services rushed to the Peabody Estate on Horseferry Road, Westminster, at 7.30am on March 14.

They had received reports that a baby had fallen from a height at a residential property.

Officers cordoned off the area and closed off traffic to allow forensic officers to examine the scene.



The police cordon has since been lifted.

A spokesperson for the London Ambulance Service said the baby was treated at the scene before being taken to a major trauma centre.

But despite the best efforts of paramedics, the 18-day-old infant was pronounced dead in hospital.



Two forensic officers could be seen working on a third-floor flat that evening.

Mourners attended the scene today, leaving flowers and a toy animal in remembrance of the young girl.

The girl had fallen to her death from her home.

The tragic incident took place on a busy central London street, home to Channel 4 Headquarters, Burberry Headquarters, and luxury apartments.

Police arrested a 43-year-old woman at the scene on suspicion of murder and she was taken into police custody.



Police are calling for anyone who witnessed the incident to call 101, quoting reference 1596/14MAR

News.title: Our town is county’s most dangerous with £2m raids…spliff-toting mums push prams in drug fog & cuckooing is rife
News.content:
PROUD onlookers line the high street in a historic English town, watching a parade of children march with banners in celebration of their county.

Many towns across Cornwall mirror the image on Saint Piran’s Day, the national day of the county, held on March 5 every year.





But while all may seem picture perfect on the main thoroughfare in Bodmin, there lies an underbelly of serious crime entrenched in its community, earning it the dubious accolade of Cornwall’s most dangerous town when taking into account the severity of crimes.

Locals live in fear as top level county lines operators silently move in to prey on the community’s most vulnerable residents, setting up ‘cuckooing’ properties to store illicit substances, cash, drugs paraphernalia and use as a base to peddle a plethora of drugs.

Its main tourist attraction Bodmin Jail has a dark past, but locals are in no doubt there is a different, more threatening kind of shadow being cast over the town today.



The older generation is well aware of the web dealers are weaving around them, not least because of the aroma wafting through the air, the needles they see in the undergrowth and the police pleas for them to share information on suspected cuckooing.

Beauty practitioner Heather Dunn, 70, said: “When I moved here 40 years ago Bodmin was a fabulous town, where everybody knew everybody, it was very quiet and it was very safe, but like many towns, it has now become somewhere we have to worry about drugs.

“I live nearby in a block of flats where a known drug dealer was living, and there was often drug paraphernalia and used needles thrown in a row of hedges outside.

“I think the reason Bodmin has this problem is because of the growth of the town and the influx of people from other areas of the country – lots of people are sent here from other areas to be homed in the social housing.



“I wouldn’t come out at night-time; there are a lot of people hanging around doing goodness knows what.”

And it’s not just a night-time problem. On the day The Sun visited the historic town, on the edge of the infamous Bodmin Moor, we approached people in the town for their views.

One young mother pushing a buggy in one hand and holding a spliff in the other, refused to be drawn into conversation, while another told us you could ‘get high walking down the high street’.



It’s a menace which truly scares some long-time residents.

Sandra Vigus, 81, was walking her dog Poppy in a local park.

The grandmother of four told The Sun: “When I was a young girl I hadn’t even heard of drugs, now it’s a horrible part of the world we’re living in, and it’s frightening to hear of the things the police have discovered in Bodmin recently.

“I fear for the younger generation and for my own grandchildren growing up when there are so many people involved in drugs and they seem to be everywhere, it’s a worrying situation.”



Local man David Hodges, 66, was walking his neighbour’s dog Prince and said: “There’s a terrible problem in Bodmin with drugs, they’re everywhere.

“They raided a property last week and found drugs, but there’s probably others.

“Young people hang around in the car park at night smoking cannabis and it absolutely stinks. The smell of it makes me want to vomit, it makes me sick to my stomach. It’s disgusting and we shouldn’t have to put up with it.”



One local woman who asked not to be identified said: “The more raids the police do, the better, this town is overrun with drugs of all kinds, they’re readily available if you know who to ask. It gets worse with every year that passes.

“You could get high just walking down the high street at whatever time of day, people smoke cannabis all the time like it’s acceptable, and there are any number of drugs dens and drug dealers ready to take advantage of vulnerable folk.”

Just weeks ago, local cops raided a ground floor flat and seized weapons, cash, and a ‘significant amount’ of suspected cocaine, MDMA, diazepam, cannabis and drugs paraphernalia.

The 38-year-old man was arrested on suspicion of possession of a class A drug, possession of a class B drug, and possession with intent to supply a class C drug. He was bailed as the investigation continued.



In January police shut down two properties housing vulnerable people who have been ‘cuckooed’.

Police say cuckooing involves criminals, usually drugs dealers, taking over a vulnerable person’s home and using it for gain, leaving the tenant frightened and trapped.

The force has previously urged residents to share information to identify other cuckooing victims, saying ‘we know cuckooing happens in Bodmin’.

Another cuckooed home was raided and shut down in September last year, and two in 2024, as county lines gangs continue to target properties in more rural areas.

For three months the only people allowed inside after a closure order is obtained are the named tenant, police officers, social workers or health professionals.





Perhaps one of the biggest successes for police in Bodmin in recent years came in 2023, when an industrial sized, multi room cannabis factory was uncovered, housing 4,000 plants worth an estimated street value of £2 million.

Four Albanian nationals awaiting immigration papers were discovered to be gardening the crop, and all were given two-year suspended sentences.

Police Inspector Adam Stonehill, who leads the Bodmin Neighbourhood Team, said: “Drugs can cause so much harm in our communities, and are integrally linked to a wide range of criminality. This is a great disruption being able to remove these illegal substances and weapons from the streets.

“I hope this shows that we are taking a robust approach against drug crime in the town.”

Crime in Bodmin increased by 7 per cent in 2025 when there was a total of 968 crimes reported compared to 906 in 2024. This translates to 87 crimes per 1,000 people, 54 per cent higher than the average Cornwall crime rate of 57 per 1,000.



Figures rank dangerous towns in the county, based on the severity of crimes.

According to Crime figures, as of 2026, Bodmin’s crime is 40% higher than the south west average and 17% higher than the England, Wales & Northern Ireland overall figure.

Crime Risk Scores rank it as the most dangerous town in Cornwall when taking into account the severity of crimes, followed by Liskeard and Newquay.



The most common crimes reported are violence and sexual offences, similar to most areas in the UK.

Cornwall Council declined to comment, saying it is a police matter.

Police Chief Inspector Matt Setchell of Devon and Cornwall Police said: “We know that drug crime is a concern across our communities and is one that can cause so much harm. That’s why we are committed to keeping the public safe by reducing offending and deterring this type of criminal activity in our towns.

“Our officers continue to carry out planned activity such as warrants, closure orders, targeted operations and investigations to put offenders before the courts.

“We’ve had some positive successes recently as part of our efforts to disrupt drug supply, and this focus will continue.



RECOMMENDED STORIES

“I’d also like to encourage the public to come forward and report information about suspected drug dealing in Bodmin. Their information is vital and we rely on reports from our community to plan out our activity and patrols to further disrupt crime on our streets, reduce the anti-social behaviour associated with drug dealing and/or use, and to protect vulnerable people who are exploited and drawn into the supply of illegal drugs.

“Information can be reported directly to us, or anonymously through independent charity Crimestoppers.”

News.title: Grandmother dies fleeing Nebraska’s largest-ever wildfires as residents told to ‘get out’ & officials fear worse to come
News.content:
A BELOVED grandmother has died while trying to flee the largest-ever wildfire to rage through her state.

Nebraska is enduring a devastating chain of four wildfires that have burned over half a million acres down to the ground, and officials fear more is to come.





State governor Jim Pillen confirmed that Rose White, 86, from Arthur in Arthur County, died in an attempt to flee the raging blaze in Morrill.

“One Nebraskan has lost her life…I won’t go into the circumstances. It hurt a little bit, but the grandma was trying to get away,” he said in a press briefing on Saturday.

“I think it’s really important as our volunteers go and make awareness in the coming days if this wind gets out of control…when you run into someone that is a thick head like me, say ‘get the heck out of here.'”

Everything else in life can be rebuilt he said, urging residents to prioritise their safety and their lives.

In her obituary, Rose’s family have called her “the smile and heart behind the camera” and a “fearless grandma that fought snakes, healed scrapes, and dried tears with love and treats.”

“She may be gone, but her memory will live on through all of her friends and family.”

Rose is survived by her four sons, four granddaughters, two grandsons, her sister, and twelve great grandchildren.

Her funeral will be held on March 20 in Arthur.



Fears are high that if strong winds pick up in the region, as they are expected to, the fires across central and western Nebraska will continue to spread.

They have already burned over 600,000 acres, more than any fire season ever seen in the state with four fires currently being monitored.

The Morrill County Fire that officials believe was ignited by an electrical fire from strong winds had razed over 450,000 acres to the ground as of Saturday morning.

Two other fires continue to rage in Cottonwood, Lincoln County, and in the Nebraska National Forest south of Halsey and Dunning.



Officials said they are investigating if the latter was caused by a federally-authorized burn that can be used as a tactic to control wildfire spread and has now been banned in the area until at least March 27.

A fourth fire is raging in Anderson Bridge, west of Valentine.

Mass evacuations have taken place and are underway in the worst-affected areas.

Rose is currently the only reported fatality.

“We’ve got a lot of work to do to be able to bring this under control,” warned senator Pete Ricketts as officials throw all the resources they have at the fires and declare a state of emergency.



“If we have a whole bunch of trucks come from Omaha and we don’t use them, thank God,” Pillen said.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“But we’re going to make sure we don’t wake up with any shoulda, coulda, woulda. We’re not sparing a penny.”

Meanwhile, 245 active wildfires spanning 547,000 acres are burning across the country, including in California, Colorado, New Mexico, Texas, Oklahoma, Mississippi and Florida.

News.title: Shock pictures show Putin’s charred ‘shadow fleet’ tanker drifting with no crew after blitz by Ukraine’s Sea Baby drones
News.content:
SHOCKING pictures show Putin’s charred “shadow fleet” tanker drifting with no crew after it was blitzed by Ukrainian drones.

The 277-metre-long Arctic Metagaz has been drifting without crew since a series of explosions blasted the vessel over a week ago.





New images of the vessel have emerged showing its blackened exterior and hole ridden hull.

It was snapped 50 nautical miles southwest of Malta.

Russia accused Ukraine of targeting the Metagaz with “uncrewed sea drones” in the Mediterranean between Libya and Malta.

The March 4 attack was launched from the Libyan coast.

Ukraine’s SBU state security service has not commented on the allegation.

The vessel has been sanctioned by the United States and the European Union for being part of Moscow’s “shadow fleet”.

Made up of ageing tankers the fleet transports Russian oil and gas around the world, skirting Western restrictions.



The wreck has been closely monitored by authorities in Malta and Italy fearing pollution.

The ship was reportedly carrying significant quantities of gas, heavy oil and diesel fuel.

“A potential spill could cause fires, cryogenic clouds lethal to marine life, and widespread and long-lasting pollution of water and the atmosphere,” WWF Italy warned in a statement.

Adding: “The affected area is of exceptional ecological value, with fragile deep-sea ecosystems and some of the highest biodiversity in the Mediterranean basin.”

Initial reports said the ship had sunk after explosions triggered a fire on board the vessel.



The Libyans said the tanker sank about 130 nautical miles north of the Libyan port of Sirte.

Russia’s transport ministry said 30 Russians were aboard the Arctic Metagaz.

They were all found “safe and sound in a lifeboat” by Malta’s armed forces, Maltese Home Affairs Minister Byron Camilleri said.

But, Malta’s transport authority last week said the wreck was still afloat.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Following Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, Kyiv has carried out a series of attacks on Russia’s sanctioned vessels using naval drones.



But nearly all of these strikes have been in the Black Sea.

News.title: Update on man found dead in Coventry bin as cops share 'distinctive' identifier
News.content:
Update on man found dead in Coventry bin as cops share 'distinctive' identifier

A member of the public found the unidentified man, believed to have been aged between 40 and 50, in Cash's Park in Coventry at about 5pm on Friday with police now trying to identify him

View 4 Images Police have released new information in the hopes of identifying a man whose body was found in a wheelie bin (Image: West Midlands Police / SWNS)

In an attempt to track down the identity of a man found dead in a wheelie bin after what's believed to be a hit-and-run incident, police have shared new information about his "distinctive" characteristics.

‌



Police are attempting to identify the man whose body was discovered in a wheelie bin in a park, and have released information about his "distinctive" tattoos. The unidentified man, thought to be between 40 and 50 years old, was found by a member of the public in Cash's Park off Daimler Road in Coventry around 5pm on Friday.

‌



The victim was located in a Coventry City Council wheelie bin with a green lid, and West Midlands Police are collaborating with the local authority to determine the original location of the bin.

‌



View 4 Images Police shared images of the bin the man was found in (Image: West Midlands Police / SWNS)

Authorities suspect he may have been struck by a vehicle before being transported to the park, and they are "carrying out urgent enquiries to establish who the man is, and where and how he died". West Midlands Police revealed details of two tattoos that they say are "distinctive".

The force shared that the man has a "tattoo of a cross with a snake wrapped around it on his back and the words 'Little Stardust', and a tattoo on his right arm saying 'nan' with a clover and the colours of the Irish flag". Detective Chief Inspector Phil Poole, who is leading the investigation, stated: "We have a large team of detectives, forensic specialists and other staff working around the clock to establish what has happened to this man, who he is, and who is responsible.

‌



"We've had several pieces of information come through to us as a direct result of our appeal, and we are following up those lines of enquiry. I really want to hear from anyone who recognises the descriptions of the tattoos on the man, as they are quite distinctive.

View 4 Images Police believe the man (Image: West Midlands Police / SWNS)

" I also really want to hear from anyone who has noticed unexplained damage on a vehicle, perhaps of a partner or relative, or anyone who's noticed a sudden change in behaviour from someone they know. If you know or are responsible for what happened to this man, I would urge you to come forward now so that we can get the answers that he deserves."

‌



Yesterday Detective Chief Inspector Poole said: "We are keeping an open mind, but we believe that this man has been hit by a vehicle, and then has been moved to the park. I urgently want to hear from anyone who believes they may have hit someone in their car yesterday, or who has noticed a change in behaviour from someone they know who drives, or who has noticed damage to a vehicle.

View 4 Images Police are appealing for anyone with any information regarding the man or a possible hit and run to come forward

"It's vital that we trace that person as quickly as possible so that we can get their account of what happened. If anyone in the area has found that their wheelie bin with a green lid has been stolen, I also want to hear from them."

Article continues below

Anyone with information, or who has dashcam or CCTV footage from the area, has been asked to get in touch via 101, quoting log 4148 of March 13. The force has also set up a dedicated page where people can send CCTV, mobile phone or dashcam footage from the area, or any other information, straight to detectives here.

News.title: Two Kent University students die in meningitis outbreak
News.content:
Two Kent University students die in meningitis outbreak

Two students at the University of Kent have died while multiple others are reported to be seriously ill in hospital following an outbreak of 'invasive' meningitis

View 2 Images The University of Kent in Canterbury (Image: Google Maps)

Two students at the University of Kent have died following an outbreak of "invasive" meningitis.

‌



Another 11 students from the Canterbury area are understood to be receiving hospital and reported to be seriously ill, the BBC reports.

‌



The UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) are contacting more than 30,000 students, their families and university staff to inform them on the outbreak. A spokesperson said antibiotics were being given to some students in Canterbury.

‌



Meningitis is a serious inflammation of the protective membranes surrounding the brain and spinal cord, usually caused by bacterial or viral infections. It spreads from person to person by respiratory droplets or throat secretions.

View 2 Images Two students at the University of Kent have died (Image: University of Kent)

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

‌



While meningitis can affect anyone, it is most common among babies, young children, teenagers, and young adults.

Symptoms - which can often appear suddenly in adults - include a high fever, severe headache, neck stiffness, nausea/vomiting, light sensitivity, and confusion. If not treated promptly, it can cause life-threatening sepsis.

The specific strain of the disease affecting students in Canterbury has not been identified.

Article continues below

Trish Mannes, UKHSA regional deputy director for the South East, said symptoms of the disease can often be confused with "a bad cold, flu, or even a hangover".

She added: "Students and staff will understandably be feeling worried about the risk of further cases; however, we would like to reassure them that close contacts of cases have been given antibiotics as a precautionary measure."

Those who experience symptoms of meningitis and septicaemia are urged to go to A&E or call 999.

News.title: Al-Quds London protest probed by police after Bobby Vylan 'death to IDF' chants
News.content:
Al-Quds London protest probed by police after Bobby Vylan 'death to IDF' chants

The Met Police confirmed they will be investigating after they were made aware of chants of 'death, death to the IDF' at protests in London today as 1,000 cops were called in

View 5 Images

Police have launched a probe into chants of "death, death to the IDF (Israel Defence Forces)" which were led by Bobby Vylan during the Al-Quds day demonstration in London today.

‌



The artist, whose real name is Pascal Robinson-Foster, is a member of the punk duo Bob Vylan. He repeated his controversial chant from last year's Glastonbury Festival while appearing at the protests as a speaker today.

‌



Those who were in the crowd appeared to join in with the chants. The Metropolitan Police said 12 arrests were made today amid the protest and counter-protest. The Met Police said: "We are aware of chanting made by a speaker at the Al-Quds protest and will be investigating.

‌



View 5 Images Protesters held flags and banners aloft at the protest (Image: PA)

"We recognise the concern footage and chanting like this causes, particularly with London's Jewish communities. When this language had been used previously we sought advice from the CPS who dtermined that there would be insufficient evidence to take a case forward."

‌



The force confirmed the investigation was in relation to the "death, death to the IDF" chant. Scotland Yard had braced for a "difficult public order" environment, with at least 1,000 officers called in to try and manage the crowd.

Assistant Commissioner Ade Adelekan said: “We saw significantly fewer people attend than we had anticipated. The restrictions and conditions meant many people chose to stay away and not to attend the protest or counter-protest. This shows our decision to apply for the ban was the right one. A static protest meant it was easier for officers to keep the two groups apart and prevent serious public disorder.

View 5 Images 1,000 police officers were drafted in to help manage the protest and counter-protest (Image: PA)

‌



“We made 12 arrests including for showing support for a proscribed organisation, affray and for threatening or abusive behaviour. We are also investigating chants made by a speaker at the Al-Quds protest. As I said from the outset, the decision to ban the protest march does not set a precedent and we will continue to consider each protest on a case-by-case basis.”

Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood granted the police’s bid for a month-long ban on the annual march organised by the Islamic Human Rights Commission (IHRC), making it the first time such a restriction on protest had been imposed since 2012. However, people could still legally assemble and take part in a so-called “static protest”.

‌



View 5 Images The Met Police confirmed 12 arrests were made during the protest and counter-protest (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

Hundre ds of people began arriving from 1pm, many holding Palestine flags and banners, some reading “Free Palestine” and “No to Israeli occupation”. One protester had a “Boom boom Tel Aviv” sign. Pictures of Iran’s late leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei were also held up, some accompanied by the message “Choose the right side of history”. Chants of “from the river to the sea” and “Israel is a terror state” could be heard.

‌



In the wake of the Bondi Beach terror attack, chief rabbi Sir Ephraim Mirvis said “from the river to the sea” and similar slogans “incite hatred” against Jews. The annual Al-Quds Day demonstration in London had drawn criticism over apparent backing for the Iranian regime after its organisers expressed support for the country’s late leader.

View 5 Images Protesters banged drums and chanted in the streets (Image: PA)

Article continues below

A crowd of counter-protesters were seen on the opposite side of the Thames during a demonstration organised by Stop The Hate. They waved Israeli flags while one sign read “Hamas is terrorist”. Police vans were parked along the road and on nearby Lambeth Bridge.

Two police boats were seen on the River Thames. It is thought to be the first time that Scotland Yard has used the river as a physical barrier to keep a large-scale protest and counter-protest apart.

All protests and counter-protests had to take place between Vauxhall and Lambeth bridges, and were permitted between 1pm and 3pm, the Metropolitan Police said. Al-Quds Day is named after the Arabic name for Jerusalem and is usually held on the last Friday of Ramadan.

News.title: Major crash sees air ambulance and 10 police vehicles swarm busy Newcastle road
News.content:
Major crash sees air ambulance and 10 police vehicles swarm busy Newcastle road

A busy road in Newcastle has been sealed off this evening following a serious crash, with specialist paramedics and police at the scene - and one person rushed to hospital

View 2 Images Multiple police cars are at the scene (Image: Chronicle Live)

At least 10 police vehicles and an air ambulance have descended on a busy street after a two-vehicle collision saw a person rushed to hospital.

‌



Specialist paramedics have been called to West Denton Way in Newcastle, where a large section of the road has been cordoned off by police. The closure came after crash involving two vehicles just before 5pm, Northumbria Police confirmed.

‌



A patient was rushed to the Royal Victoria Infirmary (RVI) in Newcastle for treatment "We received a call at 4.35pm on Sunday 15 March to reports of a road traffic incident on Northumbria Walk, Newcastle," the North East Ambulance Service said.

‌



View 2 Images Police have sealed off West Denton Way in Newcastle (Image: Chronicle Live)

"We dispatched an ambulance crew, a specialist paramedic, and an advanced paramedic practitioner to the scene. One patient was taken to the RVI for further treatment."

Article continues below

A Northumbria Police spokesperson said: "Just before 5pm today (Sunday), we received a report of a two-vehicle collision in the Northumbria Walk area of West Denton.

"Emergency services are in attendance and the road is currently closed. Road users are advised to find alternate routes where possible."

News.title: Unique International Space Station experiments will help astronauts on Mars missions
News.content:
EXCLUSIVE: Unique International Space Station experiments will help astronauts on Mars missions

The UK Space Agency and Cambridge-based firm LinkGevity are working with Lithuania-based Delta Biosciences to see how medicines react to being taken into space

View 6 Images Astronauts on a mock Mars training mission (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

A trail-blazing scientific mission to the International Space Station is to test drugs and medicines so they can be used by astronauts on future trips to Mars. Britain is teaming up with a Lithuania-based space health company for the unique new experiments. Selected medicines are being sent into space to see how they react to prolonged exposure to cosmic radiation. If successful, the medicines will be used by astronauts on trips to Mars. Scientist Dominykas Milašius said: “Mankind has always thought about the next frontier. Mars is that next one. We are trying to make sure that we can use near space as an exploration environment and potentially protect astronauts and the crew for longer periods of time as well.

‌



View 6 Images Scientist Dominykas Milašius from Delta Biosciences, a Lithuania-based space health company.

‌



NASA's menu for Artemis 2 astronauts on first manned moon mission in 54 years READ MORE :

“The UK’s historic focus on life sciences and new space is a good context in which a company like ours can contribute some of the knowledge and build missions together.”

‌



On a Mars mission which will last around three years there will be no resupply and no backup pharmacy. Every tablet and drug must survive the journey.

The UK Space Agency and Cambridge firm LinkGevity are working with Delta Biosciences, a Lithuania-based space health company. Milašius is the co-founder of Delta Biosciences and said medicines are developed and stored on Earth under the protection of the planet’s atmosphere and magnetic field.

View 6 Images Astronauts on a mock Mars training mission (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

‌



But in deep space, astronauts are exposed to galactic cosmic rays and solar particle events that can damage cells and alter molecular structures. Yet radiation does not just affect people. It can change the drugs designed to keep them alive.

He said: “Right now, if you talk about basic medicine such as adrenaline, in contact with space radiation the shelf life shortens massively. It becomes unusable in orbit rapidly. So we need to look for solutions.”

View 6 Images A mosaic of the Schiaparelli Hemisphere of the planet Mars, showing the Schiaparelli Crater, circa 1980. (Image: Getty Images)

‌



Research has shown that radiation can significantly reduce the shelf life of certain medicines. And on a Mars mission, where there is no resupply and no evacuation, the situation can become catastrophic.

“Three years would be the parameters for a potential mission to Mars,” Milašius said. “We are building towards the goal of building a medical kit for astronauts for Mars that would make sure the medicines work for that entire period.”

View 6 Images The International Space Station - where new experiments will take place (Image: ISS/NASA / SWNS)

‌



Space medicine faces a brutal reality: in deep space, drugs can weaken, fail — or chemically change. Away from Earth’s protective atmosphere, astronauts are exposed to constant and deadly cosmic radiation.

That radiation does not just damage human cells. It can hit the molecular structure of medicines themselves. Liquid emergency drugs such as adrenaline are particularly vulnerable. Under radiation, their shelf life can shrink dramatically.

View 6 Images The International Space Station - where new experiments will take place (Image: NASA/AFP via Getty Images)

Article continues below

Other medicines can slowly break down, losing potency over time. Some compounds may form new chemical byproducts as they degrade — substances that were never seen during normal Earth testing.

And antbiotics can become unstable, raising serious concerns about whether they would still fight infection properly after years in orbit. On a Mars mission lasting around three years, there is no resupply and no backup pharmacy. Every tablet and vial must survive the journey.

The experts are now stress-testing medicines in space and redesigning them with radiation-resistant formulations. This is all to ensure astronauts are not carrying a medical kit that quietly stops working millions of miles from home.

News.title: Schoolgirl, 12, feared to have taken her own life after Snapchat bullying
News.content:
Schoolgirl, 12, feared to have taken her own life after Snapchat bullying

Schoolgirl Amelia Bath, 12, was tragically found dead by her mum Marie when she went to wake her up for school - with fears she took her own life over bullying via Snapchat

View 3 Images Schoolgirl Amelia Bath who was found dead aged 12 (Image: Lisa Marsh / SWNS)

A 12-year-old girl who was tragically found dead in her bedroom is feared to have taken her own life after being bullied on Snapchat.

‌



Schoolgirl Amelia Bath was discovered by her mum Marie when she went to wake her up for school on Friday morning, March 6.

‌



The girl's family said they spent the previous evening at Amelia's grandparents house where they laughed over fish and chips, with nobody suspecting anything was wrong. After police attended the home and took Amelia's phone and iPad, Snapchat messages were discovered suggesting she was being bullied online, her uncle Kris Marsh said.

‌



Kris, 42, said: "It was an absolute massive shock to everybody. It was totally out of the blue. I don’t think you ever recover from something like this. Marie her mum had gotten up that morning as usual, gone to wake her up for school and then found Amelia in her bedroom. Her world had just been completely turned upside down.

View 3 Images Amelia Bath (second from right) with parents Steve and Marie and her sister Olivia (Image: Lisa Marsh / SWNS)

"Hours before they were all around their grandparents house having a meal, laughing and joking around. You just don’t ever expect that your world can change so drastically in a few hours. It was just a completely normal evening, you wouldn’t have known there was anything on her mind. Nothing seemed off at all, and then obviously what happened was the following morning."

‌



Amelia, who attended The Regis School in Bognor Regis, leaves behind her parents Marie and Steve Bath, as well as her older sister Olivia, aged 14, and was loved by many.

Her uncle said the family were in complete shock when police told them about the Snapchat messages as Amelia had always seemed happy and popular. They are now working to raise awareness for cyber bullying, encouraging children to speak to their families when they are struggling.

Kris added: "It is an ongoing investigation, but there were messages on Snapchat. The police have obviously taken her iPhone and her iPad to go through them and see what they can find. There were absolutely no signs. Everything on the face of it was totally normal. That’s why it is so dangerous with these apps.

‌



"These days kids seem so quick to be horrible to one another. They might think that it is just a text message or a voice message but it has lasting impact on people’s lives. “If anything our message from this whole tragic incident would be that there is always somebody you can talk to. Let your feelings be known because no 12-year-old should think that resorting to suicide is the only way out."

View 3 Images Amelia Bath (right) and her sister Olivia (Image: Lisa Marsh / SWNS)

Amelia's aunt Lisa, 39, launched a GoFundMe for her funeral costs, a donation to Chestnut Tree House, a children's hospice care charity, and for funds towards a memorial garden. The page has already raised over £15,000, and is flooded with messages of support.

‌



Kris said: "Amelia was just your normal happy go lucky, very popular little girl. She was loved by everyone. There were absolutely no signs that were any underlying issues or anything. On the face of things, she was perfectly normal. She liked her music and she was quite quirky. She was just a good, fun, little girl. She was full of life and full of energy."

Lisa wrote on the fundraiser page: "On the morning of Friday 6th March, our world shattered. My sister Marie found her precious daughter, Amelia, who had tragically taken her own life at just 12 years old. No family ever imagines facing a loss like this, and the shock and heartbreak have been overwhelming for everyone who knew and loved her - our family, friends, and her school friends and teachers.

"Amelia was a bright, funny, kind-hearted, and loving girl who filled every room with warmth and laughter. She had the most beautiful soul and so much life ahead of her. She was deeply loved, and she loved deeply in return. The silence she has left behind is unbearable, and the hole in our hearts will never truly heal."

‌



A spokesman for Sussex Police told the Argus: "Emergency services were called to an address in Yapton Lane, Walberton, just before 7am on Friday, March 6. "Sadly a 12-year-old girl was found deceased. Her next of kin are aware and our thoughts are with them at this difficult time.

"We are working alongside schools and partner agencies to support the girl’s family and others who have been impacted. Support for children, young people, parents and carers in West Sussex is available if they are struggling, worried about someone else, or are dealing with bereavement.

"Information, advice and local support can be accessed through e-wellbeing, the How Are You Really Feeling? campaign, and the West Sussex County Council mental health support page, which brings together a range of help and resources.

Article continues below

"Anyone in need of urgent mental health support should contact NHS 111 and select the mental health option. In an emergency, people should always call 999. Officers are reviewing the wider circumstances. Her death is not being treated as suspicious and the case has been referred to HM Coroner."

News.title: Man who killed pastor and pinned him to wall 'like crucifixion' makes death penalty plea
News.content:
Man who killed pastor and pinned him to wall 'like crucifixion' makes death penalty plea

WARNING: DISTRESSING CONTENT. Adam Sheafe, 51, has been charged with murder after beloved pastor William Schonemann, 76, was found dead in his home in New River, Arizona

View 3 Images Adam Sheafe, 51, admitted to murdering the beloved pastor (Image: Maricopa County Sheriff’s Office)

A man who admitted to murdering a pastor who was found dead with his hands pinned to the wall "similar to crucifixion" has made an unusual death penalty request.

William Schonemann, 76, was found dead in his home in New River, Arizona, on April 28, 2025 by members of his congregation from New River Bible Chapel who went to check on him. According to the Maricopa County Attorney's Office, the beloved pastor "had been positioned with the arms outstretched, similar to a crucifixion."

Adam Sheafe, 51, was identified as a suspect through evidence found at Pastor Schonemann's house and later charged for the pastor's murder and other crimes, as part of what authorities called a "larger plot by Sheafe to kill 14 Christian leaders around the country. But when he appeared in court on Thursday, he made an unusual request.

View 3 Images William Schonemann, 76, was a beloved pastor who was found dead by two members of his congregation (Image: William Schonemann/Facebook)

Wife killer's twisted bid to make people believe she was still alive READ MORE :

Sheafe was charged with one count each of first-degree murder, second-degree burglary, first-degree burglary, kidnapping, theft of means of transportation, second-degree criminal trespass, and three counts of attempt to commit first-degree murder. During his court appearance, Sheafe tried to plead "no contest" to the charges, but was denied by prosecutors who said that plea is usually for defendants who cannot remember their crime due to intoxication or other reasons.

He then tried to plead guilty, and even asked for the death penalty, saying that the process should be sped through the courts so everyone could "move on with our lives." Sheafe made a lengthy statement to the court, in which he said: "It's an undisputable fact that the crime was heinous in nature. I intended it to be heinous."

He added that this combined with the victim's age were "two aggravating factors", saying he has "no mitigating factors" before saying: "That's why I'm saying, why do we have to drag this on and on? Why can't we just go to sentencing. I'm not contesting anything."

View 3 Images Sheafe asked the court to expedite his trial and give him the death penalty (Image: Maricopa County Sheriff’s Office)

Sheafe said he "gave a full confession to the FBI" and referenced multiple interviews he had carried out with separate media outlets. He said: "From day one, I've said I did this. These are the reasons why I did this, and I'm not contesting anything. And my speedy trial rights went from five months to basically two and a half years. And we're dragging this out in the interest of justice. What about the victim's families? What about me? What about my family? We want closure so we can move on with our lives."

Article continues below

Sheafe's request for a bench trial and the death penalty were denied. He is due to return to court on April 24, and according to Maricopa County Superior Court case records, he is representing himself.

Sheafe is also accused of breaking into a home in Cave Creek, Arizona, about 20 miles away from New River, and stealing a pickup truck. He then reportedly used the truck in a burglary, which was caught on video, and was captured the next day after breaking into another home. Maricopa County Sheriff's Office detectives "connected Sheafe to the pastor's murder" through evidence from Pastor Schonemann's home, as well as the Cave Creek burglary, the stolen truck and items found in Sheafe's rucksack.

Sheafe admitted to killing the pastor, crucifying his body to a wall and even said he placed a crown of thorns on his head. Neighbours, friends and loved ones said Pastor Schonemann was a friendly man who lived in an area known for being so safe that people often left their doors unlocked.

News.title: Killer who stuffed mum-of-two's body in a fridge leaves family reeling with sick excuse
News.content:
Killer who stuffed mum-of-two's body in a fridge leaves family reeling with sick excuse

A mother-of-two was found stuffed in a fridge by her boyfriend after being strangled and dumped in a forest - after he apologises for the 'suffering' he has caused

View 2 Images Christopher Blevins strangled his girlfriend to death

A warped killer who stuffed his girlfriend's body in a fridge has learned his fate.

‌



Christopher Blevins, 46, strangled 50-year-old mother of two Laura Hughes to death before placing her remains in a fridge and dumping it in a forest. Her body was wrapped in a sleeping bag, secured into the fridge with straps and covered by a rug.

‌



This week, Blevins was sentenced to 17 years in prison afer pleading guilty to first-degree aggravated manslaughter.

‌



Surveillance footage and a license plate reader caught Blevins' pickup truck carrying what appeared to be a refrigerator around the time of the murder. Laura's body was discovered on 22 December 2024.

View 2 Images Laura Hughes body was found stuffed in a fridge by her boyfriend

In a bizarre statement read by his lawyer, Blevins, from New Jersey, US, said he "didn't mean for it to happen," PEOPLE reports. Apologising for the "suffering" he "casued," the killer said: "I do not know why it happened. I wish I could do things differently, in that I just left."

‌



The pathetic excuse will be another blow for Laura's distraught family, and the two children she leaves behind.

Cornel Alston, the father of Hughes' two children, spoke powerfully at the sentencing and told the killer: "Although there is no amount of time that will bring [Hughes] back, you should never be able to walk among the free. This death will never be off my children. They will carry this the rest of their lives. My kids should never have to worry about someday, their mother's killer will walk down the same side of the sidewalk."

Camden County Prosecutor Grace C. MacAulay praised the police investigations in pinning down the killer and said: "We must commend our partners at the New Jersey State Police and Cape May County Prosecutor's Office for their steadfast investigative work in State v. Christopher Blevins.

Article continues below

"Through persistence, investigators uncovered the truth behind the death of 50-year-old Laura Hughes, whose remains were discovered in Belleplain State Forest, leading to Blevins' guilty plea. Their professionalism and commitment resulted in the defendant being held accountable."

For confidential support, call the 24-hour National Domestic Abuse Freephone Helpline on 0808 2000 247 or visit womensaid.co.uk. If you or your family have lost a friend or family member through fatal domestic abuse, AAFDA (Advocacy After Fatal Domestic Abuse) can offer specialist and expert support and advocacy. For more info visit www.aafda.org.uk.

Do you have a story to share? Email niamh.kirk@reachplc.com
